# Example Scenario: Ambiguous Requirement

## Input

```
Make the link service more robust and user-friendly.
```

Deliberately vague — no concrete feature, no numeric target, two
unmeasurable adjectives ("robust", "user-friendly"). This is the kind of
requirement a stakeholder actually hands an engineer, and the assignment
explicitly requires handling it.

## Ambiguity detection (as generated by the Copilot Console)

The engine's `_detect_ambiguities` pass (`backend/app/services/copilot_engine.py`)
flags:

- `"robust"` — used without a measurable definition (no target error rate,
  uptime, or failure-mode list given).
- No quantitative targets anywhere in the requirement text at all (the
  engine checks for the presence of any digit in the input).

## Task breakdown (generic heuristic path)

| ID | Task | Depends on | AI assistance |
|---|---|---|---|
| T1 | Clarify requirement & define scope | — | AI pattern-matches likely-ambiguous terms ("robust", "user-friendly"); engineer must go back to the requester — this task cannot be skipped or guessed past. |
| T2 | Design data model / API contract | T1 | Blocked until T1 resolves what "robust" and "user-friendly" actually mean for this service. |
| T3 | Implement core logic | T2 | Same block. |
| T4 | Unit & integration tests | T3 | Same block. |
| T5 | Document & review | T3, T4 | Same block. |

## Why the engine deliberately does *not* guess

This is the most important behavior to highlight for an ambiguous input:
the Copilot Console's `final_summary.implementation_approach` for a
non-matching, under-specified requirement explicitly states *"No concrete
implementation generated — requirement did not match the supported
mandatory use case and contained insufficient detail for the generic
heuristic engine to derive one."* This is a deliberate engineering
decision, not a limitation discovered by accident: an AI-assisted tool
that silently invents a plausible-looking "robust and user-friendly"
implementation for an unspecified requirement is actively dangerous — it
produces work product that looks complete while encoding the AI's guess at
intent rather than the actual stakeholder's intent. This is exactly the
class of AI-related risk called out in `risk_analysis` for the
fully-specified use case too ("AI-generated code/tests for an
under-specified requirement can look plausible but not match actual
intent").

## What a human engineer would actually do here

1. Go back to the requester with concrete questions: *robust against what
   failure mode — DB downtime, malformed input, traffic spikes? User-
   friendly for whom — the API consumer, or an eventual end-user-facing
   redirect page?*
2. Convert answers into measurable requirements (e.g. "P99 redirect
   latency under 100ms even during a 10x traffic spike" is testable;
   "robust" is not).
3. Only then re-submit as a well-defined greenfield or brownfield
   requirement, following the same task-decomposition pattern shown in
   `greenfield_example.md` / `brownfield_example.md`.

## Output validation

There is nothing to validate at the code level for this input — that
absence is itself the correct, validated behavior. The relevant test is
`backend/tests/test_unit_copilot.py::test_generic_requirement_falls_back_to_heuristic_analysis`,
which asserts the engine returns `generated_artifacts == ["Requirement
analysis only"]` for a non-matching requirement rather than fabricating
artifacts it has no basis for.
